participation on the proposed ordinance.
sections.facts:
The City Council passed at first reading a proposed ordinance which will create a moratorium on
the conversion and sale of rental housing to tenancy-in-common ("TIC") projects in Berkeley
retroactive to April 9, 1991 [hereinafter cited as the "proposed ordinance'1. A copy of the proposed
ordinance is attached hereto as Exhibit A to Attachment 1. The proposed ordinance defines a TIC
project as the conveyance of an ownership interest in any part of a building containing four or more
residential dwelling units coupled with a written agreement made at the time of sale to exclusive
occupancy of any unit. An ownership interest is further defined as any direct or indirect fractional
undivided interest in land, including ownership as a general or limited partner, through ownership
of shares of stock, through participation in a club or other association, etc. A right of exclusive
occupancy includes a right conferred by license, lease or other means.
The ostensible purpose ofthe proposed ordinance is to restrict the conversion of certain residential
rental dwelling units to TIC projects pending a study of long range solutions to the City's housing
needs.
councilmember chandler has owned one unit of a two-unit Berkeley TIC since 1984.
Councilmember Wainwright owns seven four-unit apartment buildings in Berkeley. Both wish to
know whether they have a conflict-of-interest with respect to the proposed ordinance. A second
reading of it has been postponed while awaiting the decision of the Fair Political Practices
Commission (the "Commission").
The City Attorney,s Office of the Ci$ of Berkeley has forwarded to the Commission their
memorandum opinion of the matter, including a request for an opinion on the issue of the
reasonable foreseeability of a material financial impact on the Councilmembers, dated October 24,
1991 [hereinafter cited as the "Memorandum"]. A copy of the Memorandum is attached hereto as
Attachment 1.


Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
In the following analysis I take issue with the legal and factual bases for the Memorandum. I have
used the material attached to and cited to by the Memorandum, additional factual material supplied
by the City and others and additional judicial and regulatory decisions.
ISSUES
The legal issues presented are (1) is Councilmember Chandler disqualified under the Political
Reform Act of 1974, Chapter 7, title 9 of the California Government Code and the accompanying
regulations, from participating in or voting on the proposed ordinance due to her ownership of a
Berkeley TIC unit, and (2) is Councilmember Wainwright likewise disqualified due to her ownership
of Berkeley residential rental property?
sections.analysis:
The basic principle of The Political Reform Act of 1974 [hereinafter cited as the "Act" ] is as follows:
No public official at any level ofstate or local government shall make,
participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position
to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
to know he has a financial interest.
California Government Code $ 87100.
The general test for disqualification of an elected official for conflict-of-interest under the Act is
adequately defined in the Memorandum. For the purposes of the proposed ordinance a Berkeley
City Councilmember who has an interest in real property worth more than One Thousand Dollars
($1,000.00) should disqualifu him or herself if


Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
(1) a matter involves a financial interest of the official, and (2) the
effect of the decision is material to that financial interest, and (3) the
material financial effect is reasonabl!, foreseeable, and (4) is distin-
suishable from its effect on the oublic generally.
Memorandum, supra p. l, at 2 (emphasis in the original). See ako Government Code $$ 87100 and
87103.
There is no doubt that each of the Councilmembers has an interest in real property worth more than
One Thousand Dollars ($1,000.00). Both Councilmember Chandler and Councilmember Wainwright
have an indirect financial interest in the proposed ordinance (Compare 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18702.1 (economic interest directly involved) and Section 18702.3 (ownership interest in real property
indirectly involved). My principal disagreement with the City Attorney's analysis centers on her
discussion ofwhether the decision on the proposed ordinance will foreseeably have a material effect
on the Councilmembers' financial interests. Again, the law cited in the City Attorney's Memoran-
dum is unobjectionable: A financial interest is material if it will haye
a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of:
(A) Ten Thousand Dollars ($10,000.00) or more on the fair market
value of the real property in which the official has an interest. . . .
Memorandum, supra p. 1, at 3. See also 2 Cal. Code of Regs. $$ 18702.3(a)(3)(A) and 18702.3(c).
However, the application of the law to the facts of this case and the development of the facts
themselves is incomplete and inaccurate.
I. COUNCILMEMBER CHANDLER
By the terms of the above regulation, in order to determine if there has been a material financial
effect, we must decide if the Councilmember's participation in decisions involving the proposed
ordinance will likely contribute to financial change to the value of the Councilmember's real
property; and, if so, if that financial change equals or exceeds Ten Thousand Dollars ($10,000.00).
A. Reasonably Foreseeable Financial Effect.
The Memorandum discloses that Councilmember Chandler has been a fifty percent (50Vo)
owner/occupier ofa Berkeley duplex TIC since 1984. The value ofher half is One Hundred Forty
Thousand Dollars ($140,000.00). Memorandufi, supra p. 1, at 3. The ana$sis states, without
elaboration, that if the proposed ordinance is passed as submitted it 'theoretically" increases the
value of real estate not similarly restricted, such as Councilmember Chandler's. The City Attorney
does not come to grips with this but defers to the City Appraiser, and, ultimately, to the Commission,
to determine its likely economic effect. Memorandum, supra p. 1, at 3-5.


Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
1. Analysis by the City Appraiser.
The City Appraiser, however, has no problem initially in finding an economic effect on properties
such as Councilmember Chandler's. (See Exhibit B to Aftachment I for the City Appraiser,s
response to the City Attorney's questions on the economic effect of the proposed ordinance.)
Though not quoting a percentage or dollar increase for the TIC units exempt from the proposed
ordinance, the City Appraiser unequivocally states that
those existing TICs and condo conversions will be benefitted [by the
proposed ordinance in its current form] to the degree that future
competition, i.e., increases in the actual as well as potential supply of
such ownership dwellings is eliminated.
Exhibit B to Attachment 1, response to Question 1. A.
Furthermore,
[i]nasmuch as TIG represent the most affordable segment of the
total housing market in Berkeley . . . [d]emand remains strongest with
the smallest level of pice sensitivity.
In any event, TICs are not generally thought to be as pice sensitive as
higher piced seg,rants of the housing market, especially in a
downtuming real estate mtrket. This is because TICb are priced
substantially below non-TIC housing units having the same or similar
amenities.
/d. at response to Question 1. C. (emphasis added).
Despite these observations, the City Appraiser concluded that "nine months is sufficiently short a
time frame that there will probably be little noticeable impact". Yet he admitted at the same time
that he could find no empirical data to determine what would be the economic impact of the
moratorium created by the proposed ordinance. Exhibit B to Attachment 1, response to Question
r. D.
2. Evidence Contrary to City Appraiser's Conclusion.
a. Berkeley City Council Housing Subcommittee Report
I had no trouble in obtaining the results of empirical data on the TIC market. Firstly, the City
Council established a Housing Subcommittee, (hereinafter, the 'subcommittee"), concomitant with
its agreement to consider passing the proposed ordinance. This Subcommittee was charged with
studying the housing situation in Berkeley and developing recommendations for an affordable general
housing plan. It drew on a wealth of statistical housing data and studies in preparing its reports.


Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
First Status Report of the City Council Housing Subcommittee to the Mayor and Members of the
City Council of Berkeley (June 4, 1991), attached hereto as Attachment 2, especially Appendix D:
Data Sources [hereinafter cited as the "Report']. The Report declared that
[i]n mid-1989 there were about 1,2fi) owner-occupied TIC units in
about 550 buildings. At least lOVo and as many as 25Eo were owner'
occupied in 1980, so there were around 1,0fi) TIC conversions in the
1980s.
The majority of 1980-89 conversions were in single-family homes, and
eighty percent in one or two unit buildings.
Advertisements for TIC owner-occupancies mostly list prices in a
range between $75,000 and $175,000. This is affordable to house-
holds with incomes ranging ftom $25,000 to $50,000. For one to
three person households these prices are affordable to moderate
income buyers with incomes ranging from 8l% to 125% of median.
Report, supra, at 16-18.
In marked contrast to the affordability of TIC units to a wide range of local inhabitants,
[h]ome prices in Berkeley have increased substantially faster than in
the East Bay as a whole. In 1990 the average single-family detached
home in the Berkeley area sample tracked by the Real Estate
Research Council of Northern California was $320,000. This is
affordable to families making $90,000 a year and having at least
$64,0fi) for a downpayment and closing costs. l-ess than 10 percent
of Berkeley households have such an income.
Id. at 19.
The inescapable conclusion one derives from reading this data is that TIC units are the last bastion
of affordable home ownership in Berkeley. Even the City Appraiser admits that TICs mainly attract
" lhe +80Vo of all local households that cannot meet the income tests for more conventional home
ownership in this region." Exhibit B to Attachment 1, response to Question 1. B. And there is a
strong demand by people to live here. "Market pressures on housing costs in Berkeley are severe,
as the combination of the city's amenities and central location and the presence of the University
of California create strong demand for housing here." Report, sapra, at 15. Furthermore, as the
news media has trumpeted, home loan interest rates are at their lowest level in a decade, placing the
marginal homebuyer in his best position to afford an entry level home, such as a TIC, despite a weak
economy. See San Francisco Chronicle, November 5, 1991, at C3, col. 5 ("[E]ntry level buyers


Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
[nationwide] dominated the market in the third quarter because they are 'more focused on mortgage
rates than where the economy is headed.''). Under these circumstances, it is inconceivable that TIC
units would not be in great demand. The imposition of limits to the growth of this type of housing
necessarily fuels increases in its value. It must also be noted that many in Berkeley are aware that
temporary measures in Berkeley housing legislation often quickly wind up as permanent prohibitions.
Rent control in Berkeley, introduced in 1979-1980 as a six month moratorium on rent increases,
metamorphosed into one of the most restrictive rent control laws in the country. ,See also, partial
list of other restrictive Berkeley housing legislation, Report, supra p.5, at 15. Being alert to this,
many will bid up the value of TIC-s now, despite the proclaimed nine month limitation of the
proposed ordinance's moratorium.
b. kading Realtor in TIC Market.
Secondly, I have received information from Mr. George S. Oram, Jr., Broker/Realtor, who originated
the concept of TIC shares in the Berkeley housing market and whose company does the bulk of TIC
unit transactions in Berkeley. His data is readily available. He has summarized his findings in letters
to the Mayor of Berkeley and to the members of the Berkeley City Council. Eg., letter from George
S. Oram, Jr./Berkeley Can Do Better to Mayor Hancock and the City Councilmembers (September
25, l99l), attached hereto as Attachment 3. As a consequence, his expertise in this matter is well
known in City Hall.
Mr. Oram's observations coincide with those of the City Appraiser's noted above. In particular, he
points out that TIC demand remains strong, being the only housing in Berkeley whose value has not
declined in the past 2 years. He attributes this to the fact that TICb represent the best value for the
money in today's housing market and are, in fact, "the lowest price quality housing available in
town." In addition, he obsewes that even with an increasing supply, the demand for them is so great
that prices are not declining. Therefore, he concludes that the effect of a moratorium on future
conversions of housing to TICs can only mean a rapid increase in the prices of TICs. See
Attachment 3. As the City Appraiser is apparently basing his analysis solely on general principles
of appraising, and, as noted above, admits that the value of existing TICs will be benefitted by the
elimination of the supply of new TICs, it is a wonder that his conclusion was not enlightened by the
axiom of supply and demand driving market prices.
Additionally, Mr. Oram informs me of a critical error in the City Appraiser's assumptions regarding
the effects of the moratorium. The City Appraiser notes that the impact of the moratorium, both
positive and negative, will tend to increase as the term ofthe moratorium increases. But he assumes
that there will be no impact until the proposed ordinance actually is passed by the City Council.
Exhibit B to Attachment 1, especially response to Question 2. B. According to Mr. Oram the
moratorium has already taken effect. The market has adjusted to the possibility of a moratorium.
This is because, when the proposed ordinance does finally pass, the moratorium will be retroactive
to April 9, 1991 by its terms. As a consequence, units which do not fall under any of the exemptions
to the proposed ordinance are already suffering, while units which are exempt are receiving increased
attention. Therefore, the moratorium has already been in effect for seven months in the calculations
of the marketplace, even without formal passage by the City Council. As it is likely to be another
month or longer before passage of the measure, the effect is of an 18 month, not a 9 month
moratorium on TICs. Even the City Appraiser agrees that 18 months is "a time frame that would


Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
be noticeable in terms of the impact on the value of properties.' Exhibit B to Attachment 1,
response to Question 1. D. The inescapable conclusion is that Councilmember Chandler's TIC will
see, and, perhaps, already has seen, an economic benefit as a result of the proposed ordinance.
3. Analysis by City Attorney.
The City Attorney notes that in the leading opinion on "reasonable foreseeability", In re Thomer,
the Commission indicated that the concept 'reasonably foreseeable' as used in Government Code
Section 87103 means more than mere possibility or remote likelihood, but less than certainty; and
that for the purpose of the Commission's deliberations on disqualification of a public official '[tlhe
ultimate test is whether the element of foreseeability . . . is present to the point that the official's
"unqualified devotion to his public duty" might be impaired.' Memorandum, supra p. l, at 4
(alterations and deletions in original) (quoting /n re Thomer, T FPPC Ops. 198,206(1975)). Having
discovered this standard, the City Attorney inexplicably also cites to /z re Gillmor for the proposition
that "reasonably foreseeable" relates to the "'anticipat[ion] and intenItion] that redevelopment will
have a financial impact on real property and business[es] located in and near the redevelopment
zone.' " Memor andum, supra p. l, at 4 (quoting In re Gillmor,3 FPPC Ops. 38' 4l (1977)).
Armed with both a general definition and an unrelated specific definition of "reasonably
foreseeable", the City Attorney looks to the City Appraiser's opinion, discussed szpra at section I.A.,
and concludes that "we are unclear as to whether the impact . . . is remote and speculative, and seek
your guidance in this regard." Memorandum, supra p. l, at 5.
4. Rebuttal of City Attorney's Analysis.
It is little wonder that the City Attorney is unclear on the reasonable foreseeability of the financial
impact. As 1n re Thomer strongly cautions, "In the last analysis, what is reasonably foreseeable must
depend on the facts and circumstances of each specific situation.' In re Thomer,l FPPC Ops. 198,
207 (1975). Her citation to In re Gillmor is inapposite on these facts. That opinion involved
rezoning a specific core area of a city for the purpose of redevelopment. Our situation deals with
prohibiting further devetopment city-wide with the purpose of aiding consideration of general
housing policy. Also, the key issue in In re Gillmor was whether the public official had "a 'financial
interest' in this decision." In re Gillmor, 3 FPPC Ops. 38, 39 (1,977). With the value of
Councilmember Chandler's real property directly affected by the proposed ordinance, there is no
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr, old; CONCLUSION appears before QUESTION

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: here of her "financial interest.' Thus, it makes no sense to supplement the definition
expounded in In re Thomer with that of In re Gillmor. Such a combination must inevitably lead to
conundrum.
Additionally, as my discussion suprc in section I.A.2. shows, the City Appraiser's analysis is riddled
with inadequacies and inaccuracios. Application of an inadequately or inappropriately defined legal
principle to a set of incomplete and inaccurate facts has led the City Attorney to the obscurity of
which she complains.
In fact, no such confusion exists. The,In re Thomer standard tells us that "reasonably foreseeable"
is delimited by mere possibility or remote likelihood on the one hand and certainty on the other.
The opinion of the Commission on this point was principally instructed by a U.S. Supreme Court


Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
case dealing with conflict-of-interest regarding a government contract. The language in that case
which caught the Commission's eye was "substantial orobability", "misht be likely" and "li\glihgAd ".
In re Thomer, 1 FPPC Ops. 198,2M (1975) (emphasis in the original) (quoting United States v.
Valley Generating Company, 364 U.S. 520, 555 (1961)). It was these terms that the
Commission had in mind when they were defining 'reasonably foreseeable".
On the facts ofour case it is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed ordinance will have a financial
effect on the TIC unit owned by Councilmember Chandler. There is a substantial probability that
the decision to ban conversions of rental housing to TIC units retroactive to April 9, 1991 will
increase the value of her property, since future stock of this highly desirable commodity will be
eliminated or deferred. Discussions supra, at section I.A.2. and infta, at section I.B. Decisions
concerning the exemptions to be contained in the ordinance and its length of operation are also
likely to have an impact on its value. For these reasons the Councilmember's "unqualified devotion
to . . . [her] public duty" must be questioned. Therefore, there is no uncertainty that it is reasonably
foreseeable that the proposed ordinance will have a financial impact on Councilmember Chandler's
property. This is not the point on which the Commission's opinion is required.
B. Material Financial Effect.
The statute requires a financial effect ofTen Thousand Dollars ($10,000.00) on the fair market value
of the real property in which the official has an interest in order for the financial effect to be
"material". 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sections 18702.3(a)(3)(a) and 18702.3(c). For Councilmember
Chandler's TIC unit to appreciate by Ten Thousand Dollars ($10,000.00) it would only have to see
an appreciation of approximately 7Vo during the course of the moratorium. Given the conclusions
of the Subcommittee and Mr. Oram noted above, it is unlikely under the circumstances that such
appreciation will not be reached and exceeded.
It should be noted that if the proposed ordinance has no exemptions Councilmember Chandler's
profit would rise even more sharply than under the previous case. Since the proposed ordinance
only bans conversion of housing stock to TICs, already existing TICs would remain outside of its
control. As the data points out, there are only about 1200 TIC units extant. As Mr. Oram notes,
most people move only every 5 to 10 years. Limiting conversion to buildings of three units or less,
as now contemplated, means that only 25 to 50 new TIC units will enter the market in a given year.
If even these are eliminated, all that is left is the turnover of the existing stock. It is an axiom of
both economics and appraising that if one curtails the supply and the demand remains constant,
prices go up. Cf. De Luz Homzs, Inc. v. County of San Diego,45 C.2d 546,562,29OP.2d 544 (1955)
(desirability drives value). Therefore, the more restrictive the ordinance the more sharply the prices
of existing TIC units will rise. ,See Attachment 3.
C. Effect on the Public Generally.
The City Attorney urges that even if a material financial effect is found, Councilmember Chandler


Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
would still be exempt from the sweep of the Political Reform Act of 1974 because the decision
affects her financial interests in the same manner as it does the general public. Memorandtm, supra
p. 1, at 5-6. Regardless, even assuming for the sake of argument that this is true, she should still be
disqualified from voting on this issue. I believe this is the issue confronting the Commission in this
situation.
The prohibitions of the Act are meant to stem not just actual fraud or unfairness by elected officials,
but the appearance of the same.
[Tlhe whole purpose of the Political Reform Act of 1974 is to
preclude a government official from participating in decisions where
it appears he may not be totally objective. . . .
It is not just actual impropieties which the law seel<s to forestall but also
the appearance of possible impropieties.
Wirt v. Morrow,70 Cal.App.3d 817,822-823, 139 Cal.Rptr. 1,61, 164-165 (197?) (emphasis added).
In an analogous case, involving the appearance of unfairness by members ofa city council voting on
a development project, the court buttressed its opinion with these statements of principle:
"It is fundamental that both unfairness and the appearance of
unfairness should be avoided. Wherever there may be reasonable
suspicion of unfairness, it is best to disquali!.' [Citation]
In Swift v. Island County (1976) 87 Wash.2d 348,552 P.zd 175, 1,83,
in declaring invalid a decision made by an administrative agency, the
court remarked: '[Wle conclude that the deliberations and vote cast
by the board of county commissioners, are void for lacking an
'appearance of unfairness.' This doctrine has been developed to
preserve the highest public confidence in those governmental
processes which bring about zoning changes or which formulate
property use and land planning mcasures.' (Emphasis added.)
Woodland Hills Residents Ass'n, Inc. v. City Council of rhe City of Los Angeles,90 Cal.App.3d 678,694,
153 Cal.Rptr. 651, 694 (1979) (citation omitted) (emphasis in the original). Cf. 55-86, 26
Op.Cal.Atty.Gen. 5, 6 (County supewisor who owns land needed by County is disqualified from
participating in the prior deliberations and decision regarding its purchase); 55-82, 26
Op.Cal.Atty.Gen. 111, 113 (Even indirect personal interest of public official in matter before him
is enough to disqualify him.)
Furthermore, it has always been the strong policy of this Commission to ensure that public
confidence in elected officials remains high by strictly enforcing the conflict-of-interest rules of
Government Code Sections 87100-87103.5 and the accompanying regulations. .lee, e.g., FPPC


Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
Page l0
Enforcement Decisions Kile Moryan 851277 (1987\ (elected ofiicial's vote had a reasonable chance
of increasing the value of his real property); Roben Garcwood 86,ry2 (1988) (failure to disclose
interest of client of his company); Mary Nichols 81/08 (1982) (failure to disclose interest of spouse's
separate property); and Sandra Smoley 861370 (1989) (failure to disclose interest of spouse's separate
property, further removed by pre-nuptial agreement).
It is against this background of decisions and principles that the situation of Councilmember
Chandler must be considered. She owns a type of housing which has been singled out by the
proposed ordinance. All TICS only account for approximately 2.5Vo oI the Berkeley housing stock,
not including student housing. See Report, sapra p.5, at 13. Ifthe proposed ordinance passes it will
directly affect the value of her property by eliminating the competition from potential new TIC units
coming on the market at a time when the demand for them is at a peak. The value of her property
will be greatly increased. Her situation is in the public eye, having been announced and discussed
at publicly broadcast City Council meetings (Radio Station KPFB-FM live broadcast of Berkeley City
Council meetings of September 24, 1991 and October 1, 1991) (See also, Legal Point Postpones
Council Decision on TICs, Berkeley Voice, September 26, 1991, at 1, col. I (Headline)). It strains
credutity to think that Councilmember Chandler's situation doesn't raise serious questions in the
public's mind on her self-interest in this matter.
The failure of the Commission to disqualiff her will be perceived as the allowance of an unfair and
improper participation in a matter of financial self-interest to the Councilmember. In order to
preserve the confidence of the public in the deliberations and decisions of the City Council,
Councilmember Chandler must be disqualified from voting on this matter or taking part in any of
the deliberations concerning it.
D. Conclusion.
For the above reasons, Councilmember Chandler's interest in a TIC housing unit will cause her
decision on the matter in question to have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her.
Because of her direct self-interest in the proposed ordinance, any action on or participation in its
passage on her part would be improper and unfair. She should, therefore, be disqualified from
voting on this issue or participating in any deliberations touching on it.
II. COUNCILMEMBER WAINWRIGHT
The same basic analysis followed above applies to Councilmember Wainwright's situation. However,
because of the substantially different facts involved, it will be seen that she has no conflict of interest
on this legislation and she should not be disqualified from participating in or voting on it.
A. Reasonablv Foreseeable Financial Effect.
The Memorandum states that Councilmember Wainwright owns seven four-unit rental buildings in
Berkeley. Memorandum, supra p. l, at 4. There is no discussion of whether or not the proposed
ordinance is likely to have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on her properties. The City
Appraiser's response to the question of whether the fair market value of property covered by the


Fair Political Practices Commisston
November 7, 1991
moratorium will suffer any foreseeable loss by being subject to the moratorium is that those
properties most suitable for conversion into TICs will 'probably loose [sic] from l5Vo to25% of their
value, absent the alternative of TIC. The least desirable probably loose [sic] from zero to l0Vo of
their potential market value." Exhibit B to Attachment 1, response to Question 2. A. No supporting
data for these conclusions is given.
A serious error in the City Appraiser's reasoning is his failure to take into account the impact ofthe
implementation of the Searle decision on Berkeley's rental property. See Dagmar Searle v. City of
Be*eley Rent Stabilbation Board,222 Cal.App.3d 131,271 Cal.Rptr. 437 (1990). After a year's worth
of study and struggle, the Berkeley Rent Stabilization Board made their decision on this matter
public the month prior to the above responses by the City Appraiser. Regulations of the Berkeley
Rent Stabilization Board were amended to conform to the requirements of the Sedrle decision. The
result is that for most landlords their rental income will be going up by 29Vo within the next six
months. (I-andlords who have previously received Individual Rent Adjustments at the hands of the
Rent Stabilization Board will have their increases adjusted accordingly). Added to this is the annual
general rent adjustment, set this year (tor 1992) at Twenty-four Dollars ($24.ffi) per month per unit.
The net result is an increase of better than 33Vo of current rents. This places Berkeley rents on a
par with fair market rents in Oakland and other surrounding communities, after lagging behind them
by 25-35% for most of the last decade. See lrtter from Stephen Barton to the Council Housing
Subcommittee (July 5, 1991), attached hereto as Attachment 4, especially pages 1-2, Table 2.
It is conceded by the City Appraiser that "the incentive for . . . [a rental property] owner to sell in
a TIC situation is lessened to the extent that the property is generating a reasonable income and rate
of return." Exhibit B to Attachment 1, response to Question 2. C. Councilmember Wainwright has
some units which are rented to Section 8 tenants (not subject to rent control) and others which are
subject to rent control. Memorandum, supra p. l, at 4. The City Appraiser opines that the loss in
value to Section 8 units under this moratorium is mitigated, since they exceed the rents allowed
under rent control. Now that rents will be at or near market rates for most rent-controlled units,
both the loss of value and the incentive to sell or convert existing rental property is eliminated. No
landlord, absent pressing circumstances, is going to spend thousands of dollars in renovations
correcting rent control caused deferred maintenance to get his units ready for sale in the TIC market
when he can get a fair return on his investment from continuing his present operations.
Reinforcing this conclusion is the practice of valuing income properties by looking to their current
rents and projecting the income stream into the future. The higher the current rents, in general, the
greater the value of the rental property. See generally Assessor's Handbook, General Appraisal
Manual (AH 501). Since the Searle mandated increases, as implemented by the Rent Stabilization
Board, don't become fully phased in until May of 1992, landlords will not wish to sell until at least
then, even if they do have a compelling reason to do so, in order to derive the greatest potential gain
upon resale. If the proposed ordinance is passed soon, its nine month term will be nearly over by
that time. Therefore, the loss in value to rental units such as Councilmember Wainwright's should
be negligible under this moratorium. Rental units which are renting at their fair market rents both
bring the rental property up to its full fair market value and give the landlord little incentive to sell
or convert to TICS to get out from under Berkeley's rent control ordinance. The effect of the TIC
moratorium is so overshadowed by this event, that its economic effect will be negligible for rental
property owners.


Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, l99l
Because there is no reasonably foreseeable financial effect for rental property owners such as
Councilmember Wainwright, she should not be disqualified from participating in or voting on the
proposed ordinance.
B. Material Financial Effect.
The Memorandum does not discuss whether Councilmember Wainwright will have a financial
consequence of Ten Thousand Dollars ($10,000.00) or more as a result of the moratorium. Rather,
it seeks the advice of the Commission on this. For the reasons I have stated above, a material
financial effect is unlikely in Councilmember Wainwright's situation.
C. Effect on the Public Generall),.
Even assuming that Councilmember Wainwright would suffer a reasonably foreseeable material
financial loss to her real property interests, this would be no different than the effect suffered by all
other landlords in Berkeley, who are mzmbers of the general public for the purpose of determining this
conflict-of-interest. Officials who are financially affected in the same manner as the general public,
or a significant segment thereof, by a governmental decision they are participating in are exempt
from disqualification, despite the fact that the reasonable foreseeable effect of their decision is a
material financial effect on them. 2 Cal.Code of Regs. Sections 18702(c) and 18703.
The City Attorney sees much in In re Fenaro,4 FPPC Ops. 62 (1978), to cast Councilmember
Wainwright as a member of an industry and, therefore, as not part of a significant segment of the
public. Memorandum, supra p. 1,at6-7. Seealso2 Cal. Code of Regs. 18703. But she overlooks
In re Oventreet,6 FPPC Ops. 12 (1981), which is controlling on these facts. The Ferraro opinion
focused on the threshold offinancial interest necessary for purposes ofdeciding whether an official's
interest is the same as a significant segment of the general public ("three or fewer rental property
units'). In re Ferraro,4FPPC Ops. 62, 66 (1978). Councilmember Wainwright's situation, as in the
Ovemteet opinion, deals with additional grounds for finding an official's interest the same as a
significant segment of the general public, even when that threshold has been reached. In re
Overstreet,6 FPPC Ops. 12, l8 (1981).
Mr. Overstreet was both a rental property owner, having a thirty-seven percent partnership interest
in 164 rental units in 26 separate properties, and a member of the Rent Stabilization Bo.ard of the
City of Berkeley . Id. at 12. Though it was conceivable that in this latter capacity he would make
decisions which would affect his financial interests, the Commission held that Mr. Overstreet "is not
disqualified from participating in any decision which will have a material financial effect on . . . [his
real property interests], if the decision will have a similar effect throughout the Berkeley rental
property industry." Id.at2l. The Commission specifically found that 'the rental property industry
in the City of Berkeley is a significant segment of the public generally." Id. at2l. Councilmember
Wainwright, as an owner of more than four rental units, is also part of the rental property industry
under the standard set forth in the Ferraro opinion and adopted in the Overstreet opinion. In re
Fenaro,2 FPPC Ops. 62,66 (1978); In re Overstreet,6 FPPC Ops. 12, 17-18.
The reasoning used by the Commission in reaching its holding in In re Overstree, was that implicit
in the Berkeley Rent Control Ordinance was the recognition that both tenants and landlords would


Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
be on the Board, representing their opposing viewpoints. Under 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sections
187O2(c) and 18703(d) an industry will be considered a significant segment of the public, and
therefore not subject to disqualification though found to have a material financial interest, if the law
authorizing the office implicitly declares this to be the case "taking into account the language ofthe
statute, ordinance or other provision of lw [sic] creating or authorizing the creation of the agency
. . , and any other relevant circumstance.'
The only requirements for being a City Councilmember in Berkeley is that you be a citizen of the
United States and a qualified elector of the State of California and of the City of Berkeley and that
you reside in the District of the City that you serve for at least thirty days prior to filing your
candidacy and at all times during your incumbency. See Berkeley City Charter Art. V, Sections 9
and 10.
In my opinion, however, the political reality of Berkeley is that the City Council has been divided
for the last ten years between members of the Berkeley Citizen's Action (the 'B.C.A.') party and
moderate democratic groups. The BCA party stands for the proposition that private ownership of
real property is an evil which must be punished. C/. Denton, Council Watch, Council of
Neighborhood Associations Newsletter (Berkeley) (angry letter from homeowner directed at BCA
policies), July/August 1991, Number 113, at 1, col. 3; partial list of restrictive housing legislation
enacted during domination ofthe Berkeley City Council by the BCA party, Report, sapra p.5, at 15.
It is believed that five or six of the current Councilmembers adhere to the BCA position in whole
or in part. In their manifesto, The City's Weahh, they explain their program, declaring that
Ir]adical groups in Berkeley have long emphasized housing programs
as key to controlling the city's wealth. There are a number of sound
strategic reasons for this. By far the largest portion of the city's
private land is used for housing. A substantial part of all personal
income is spent on housing. Within the urban economy, the accruing
capital of the community tends to be concentrated in the increasing
value of land and real estate. Finally, housing can be controlled far
more easily than other forms of wealth which are moveable and
beyond the scope of legal regulation by the city.
The City's Wealth, page 19.
Opposing this program there have always been members of moderate political groups on the City
Council who have attempted to restrain the excesses of the majority in their attack on private
property rights. Generally, such Councilmembers are elected from the wealthier hill areas of
Berkeley by those who see a need to have a representative who will protect their interests on such
a vital matter. Councilmember Wainwright is a duly elected representative of one such district. It
is implicit in the Berkeley political scene ofthe last decade as it relates to the Cig Council that there
will be representatives of these two opposing viewpoints on private proporty rights on the Cig
Council. It is this set of circumstances that makes those who own rental property in Berkeley a
"significant segment of the public." Therefore, Councilmember Wainwright's interests as a rental
property owner will be financially affected in substantially the same manner as will a significant
segment of the public. The conclusion is that she should not be disqualified from acting on the


Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
proposed ordinance, even supposing that it did have a material financial effect on her real property
interests. Government Code Section 87103 and 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sections 18702(c) and 18703.
Also, it must be pointed out that there is nothing in the situation of a rental property owner that
connects his or her vote on a TIC moratorium to any hint of unfairness or bias in the public mind,
especially under the current changed situation for them discussed supra at section II. A.
The same cannot be said for Councilmember Chandler. As an owner ofthe most desirable type of
housing in Berkeley today, an existing TIC duplex unit, she belongs to a group of approximately one-
half of one percent (O.SVo) of all Berkeley homeowners. See Report, supra p. 5, at 13, 16-17 . As
such, her interest in the proposed ordinance is not coincident with the public as a whole or a
significant segment of the public. Tlte Fenaro opinion depended heavily on the characteristics of size
and diversity in defining what constituted a significant segment of the general public. It rationalized
that a large enough mix of people of heterogeneous qualities was analogous to the public as a whole.
In re Ferraro,4 FPPC Ops. 62,67 (1978). It used as an analory the investors in the stock market.
Id. at 67. But a distinction was drawn between the indMdual, owning one investment, versus the sum
of investors in all like investments. A shareholder, under this conflict-of-interest analogy, shouldn't
be allowed to vote on decisions materially affecting only the one company in which he or she has
shares. However, the shareholder wouldn't be disqualified from a decision affecting in the same
manner all or most owners of common stock, such as one involving a tax on corporate dividends.
Id. at 67 n.4. Councilmember Chandler's situation is more like the former than the latter. She is
not like most homeowners or households in Berkeley. The market has placed her in a situation
shared only by a tiny minority. If she wishes to vote on an increase in the city property tax, there
is no conflict, as she is one among many like-affected homeowners. But as an investor in an existing
TIC she should not be allowed to participate in or vote on any decision affecting the fortunes of TIC
owners, present and potential.
D. Conclusion
Under the circumstances existing for Berkeley rental property today a rental property owner has no
financial interest in a moratorium on conversion of rental property to TIC units. It would be
uneconomic and against his or her long-term interests as an investor to convert at present.
Therefore, Councilmember Wainwright's ownership of rental property should not be a bar to her
participation in a decision on the proposed ordinance. Furthermore, even if a material interest were
reasonably foreseeable, because her situation is the same as a significant segment of the general
public, she is exempt from disqualification on that basis.
I would appreciate your thoughtful consideration ofthe issues I have raised in this letter. Thank you
for your assistance.
ResBectfully,submitted,
/L,l*-T /(-L_
Robert L. Kish
Attachments
cc:. Client

CONCLUSION: Councilmember Chandler is a member of a minuscule portion of the Berkeley populous, who
economic conditions have placed in a highly advantageous position. The proposed ordinance will
have a direct bearing on the value of her TIC investment. The general public is sharply aware of
this situation. Under these circumstances it would be improper and unfair to allow her to participate
in or vote on any decision relating to the proposed ordinance. I request that the Commission
disqualifi her from taking part any further in this matter and take such further action as it deems
necessary.
Councilmember Wainwright, under current economic and regulatory conditions, should not be
financially affected by the proposed ordinance. But even if she were, she would not be disqualified
from voting on it because her situation is affected in the same way as a significant segment of the
general public. Therefore, I request that the Commission not disquaHry her from further
participation on the proposed ordinance.
